evals.prompt: Diagnose my innovation capital across the four sources. Build my 6-month plan. Context: I'm a Senior Director leading a practice of 50 in a consulting firm. I've published a few articles, network is strong inside the firm but thin outside, well-respected for technical depth, mediocre at presenting innovation work to senior leaders.
evals.expected_output: Should structure the four-source diagnostic with 6-month plan. Human capital: STRONG — 50-person practice leadership, technical respect; opportunity: more visible track record beyond firm. Plan: 1-2 strategic external speaking engagements (industry conferences in your domain), 1 published thought-leadership piece per quarter, formal credential acquisition if relevant gap (executive program, board certification). Social capital: MIXED — strong internal, thin external. Plan: monthly cross-firm peer relationships (other practice leaders at peer firms, analysts, customer practitioners), quarterly industry-association engagement, board-style relationships with 3-5 strategic clients beyond engagement scope. Reputation capital: STRONG on technical, OPPORTUNITY on innovation/leadership. Plan: choose what you want to be known for in the next role (e.g., 'composable DXP innovation' or 'innovation operating models'); reinforce with publishing, speaking, declining work that's off-brand; cultivate third-party amplifiers (analysts, peers, clients quoting you). Impression amplifiers: WEAKEST — mediocre at presenting innovation work. Plan: invest 4-6 hours/week in demo design, prototyping for executive presentations, narrative-arc development; coach with a presentation expert; build a vivid demo for the next 3 funding asks. Note: the impression amplifier gap is the highest-leverage fix because it compounds across every internal funding ask. Cross-link with leadership-people-leader for general leadership growth.

evals.prompt: Our team's reviews have become polite and useless. Re-establish candor culture.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose first: what was punished recently? What's the current norm — silence, agreement, or polite disagreement? Likely cause: a previous candor moment had political consequence (sponsor took offense, peer was punished, reviewer's career took hit), and the team has learned silence is safer. Re-establishment moves: (1) Model it first — leader gives critical feedback on their own work publicly, names the move out loud ('this is what candor looks like; I'm asking you to do the same'); (2) Reward the speaker — first person who gives hard feedback gets public thanks; not just acceptance; (3) Punish silence, not honesty — surface what wasn't said; ask 'what didn't get raised that should have?'; (4) Use 'gentle blunt' — direct content, careful delivery; teach the team the move; (5) Separate the meeting from the decision — feedback in one venue (Brain Trust), decision in another (steering), with time between; (6) Build the Brain Trust mechanism (Catmull/Pixar) — peer review without authority, on a regular cadence, with explicit candor norms; (7) Norm of separating idea from person — repeat language: 'the idea isn't ready' not 'you didn't do enough,' 'we're killing this bet' not 'the team failed.' Time to install: 2-3 quarters. Faster recovery isn't realistic; teams that lost candor lost trust, and trust rebuilds slowly. Coach: be patient with the team; resist demanding candor before safety is rebuilt.

evals.prompt: Coach me on the demo for tomorrow's funding meeting. The CFO is skeptical.
evals.expected_output: Should structure 24-hour demo prep with explicit attention to impression amplifiers (the most underrated source of innovation capital with skeptical CFOs). (1) Lead with vivid evidence — customer voice (real quote, real video clip), prototype interaction, behavioral data (sign-ups, money committed, conversion); not slides about strategy; (2) Open with the question the CFO is asking, in CFO language — 'You're asking whether $X over Y months produces value the board can defend. Here's the case'; (3) Show the kill economics first — what's at risk per gate, what residual value at each kill point; CFOs respond to the clarity of staged commitment; (4) Use real options language for H3 work, not NPV — 'this is an option on a future business, not a discounted-cash-flow business case'; (5) Bring a customer (live or recorded) — nothing beats a customer in the room saying 'I will buy this when it's available'; (6) Close with the decision ask — clear, specific, time-bound; not vague 'continued support'; (7) Anticipate 3 hardest objections and have one-line responses ready: 'NPV is negative,' 'no revenue yet,' 'team is too small to scale.' Recommendation for tonight: do NOT spend time on the deck; spend it on the demo and the customer evidence. Bermon-specific note: your behavioral economics background helps here — frame the bet in loss-aversion terms (cost of NOT investing if the option becomes essential) where appropriate. Reference Innovation Capital book's emphasis on impression amplifiers.

evals.prompt: Our innovation lead is exhausted from playing protector with no support. Design structural protection.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the protector dynamic: innovation leaders sit between team and org, taking political hits to keep the work alive, often without explicit charter or backing. When sustained without structural protection, leads burn out. Structural protection elements: (1) Charter clarity — written mandate, decision rights, scope boundaries that the lead can point to when org pressure encroaches; (2) Executive sponsor visibility — sponsor publicly backs the lead at quarterly forums, not just privately; sponsor takes the political hit when needed, not the lead; (3) Successor sponsor — named in writing, cultivated; reduces lead's anxiety about sponsor exit; (4) Multi-year funding — written, not verbal; reduces lead's quarterly fundraising burden; (5) Co-leader or chief-of-staff — for labs >20 people, the lead can't carry alone; second-in-command handles ops while lead handles strategy and politics; (6) External advocate — analyst, board member, peer-firm leader who validates the lead's work outwardly; (7) Sabbatical / recovery time — quarterly low-meeting weeks built into the calendar; annual 2-week sabbatical recommended at 18-month mark; (8) Coach for the lead — innovation leadership is hard; external coach addresses the loneliness pattern. Coordinate with leadership-people-leader (general leadership coaching) and consulting-management-consultant (org structure if reorg is implied).

evals.prompt: Build a Brain Trust mechanism for our composable DXP service portfolio reviews.
evals.expected_output: Should adapt Catmull's Brain Trust pattern to a service-portfolio context. (1) Composition — 5-7 senior practitioners from across the practice + 1-2 cross-practice peers (CX, brand, software engineering); deliberately NO direct authority over reviewed work (this is the load-bearing element — without authority, candor survives; with authority, the Brain Trust becomes a steering committee); (2) Cadence — quarterly review of major service-portfolio bets and active engagements at risk; (3) Norms — critique the work, not the person; name problems, not solutions; everyone gives feedback (no hierarchy of speakers); reviewers take care to separate idea from person; (4) Format — 30 min context from work-owner, 45 min Brain Trust feedback, 15 min summary by work-owner of what they heard; no decisions made in the room; (5) Decision rights remain with the work-owner — Brain Trust gives input; owner decides; (6) Repetition — every major bet goes through 2-3 times across its lifecycle; the trust is built through repetition; (7) Protection — Brain Trust members must be politically protected from fallout for honest reviews; sponsor commits to this in writing; (8) Postmortem ritual — every closed bet (won or lost) gets a punishment-free postmortem with the Brain Trust. Recommend: don't call it 'Brain Trust' if that name is loaded; pick organic name. The mechanism matters more than the label. Time to install trust: 4-6 quarters. Reference Catmull's repeated insight that mechanisms beat principles.

evals.prompt: Our innovation work keeps getting credited to other teams. Diagnose the reputation problem.
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the reputation-attribution failure: the innovation team produces work but doesn't establish reputation as the source. Common patterns: (1) Storyteller deficit — no one on the team is amplifying outward; output is invisible or attributed to the absorbing BU; (2) Sponsor takes credit — common pattern; the sponsor needs visibility for political reasons; the team supplies the work; (3) Output handoff is anonymous — work delivered to BUs without team-attribution baked in; (4) Brand-flat — team has no distinctive brand within the org, so output gets attributed to whoever has stronger brand; (5) Capital-deficit on impression amplifiers — team has the substance but not the surface presence. Recommendations: (1) Storyteller role formalized — quarterly outputs with team attribution (whitepapers, blog posts, demo videos, customer stories); (2) Co-credit ritual — when work transfers to BU, both teams visibly co-own; sponsor supports this; (3) Internal speaking — team members visible at company forums, not just delivering reports; (4) External validation — external speaking, publications, analyst conversations; brings reputation back inside; (5) Sponsor conversation — explicit ask: 'we need to be visibly credited for this work; can you back that?' If sponsor refuses, that's a sponsor problem, surface to leadership. Cross-link with brand-strategist for team-brand work and marketing-copywriter for amplification content.

evals.prompt: Our team hides bad news from leadership. They're afraid to surface invalidated bets. How do I fix it?
evals.expected_output: Should diagnose the safety-and-candor failure: hiding bad news = invalidations being treated as failures, not learning. Likely causes: (1) Past bad news was punished (someone got blamed for an invalidated bet); (2) Career incentives reward 'wins' not 'learning'; (3) Sponsor takes invalidations personally; (4) No kill ceremony — kills are pure loss, not learning capture. Fixes: (1) Re-establish norm — invalidations are valuable, save future bad spend; quantify the avoided cost ('invalidating this assumption saved us $X we won't waste'); (2) Public celebration of fastest invalidation — quarterly award or recognition; the person/team who invalidated the riskiest assumption fastest is the hero, not the team that 'won'; (3) Sponsor coaching — the sponsor's reaction to invalidation sets the norm; coach the sponsor to respond with curiosity and gratitude, not disappointment; if sponsor can't change, surface as leadership issue; (4) Kill ceremony — every killed bet gets a postmortem ritual that celebrates team and learning; without ritual, kills feel like punishment; (5) Career incentives — Career Navigator metrics for innovation roles should include learning velocity and invalidation count, not just 'shipped' projects; (6) Leader vulnerability — you publicly own your own invalidated bets first; teams follow what they see, not what they're told. Time to fix: 2-4 quarters. Reference Catmull on candor culture and Innovation Capital on impression amplifiers (turn invalidations into vivid stories that build reputation, not bury them).

evals.prompt: I'm a new innovation lead. The team I inherited has been there 3+ years, has its own dynamics, and is skeptical of me. What's my first 90 days?
evals.expected_output: Should structure 90-day plan with attention to innovation-specific dynamics. Days 1-30 (Listen): (1) 1:1 with every team member, focus on what they're building, what's working, what's broken, what they need from a leader; resist the urge to direct or change; (2) Sit in on existing rituals — Brain Trusts, postmortems, demos — observe culture; (3) Meet the sponsor and successor sponsor; understand their expectations and where they back vs. constrain; (4) Map the org's actual decision graph — who decides on innovation work beyond the org chart; (5) Diagnose the lab's archetype, mandate, KPIs, charter quality (likely some gaps; document, don't fix yet). Days 31-60 (Diagnose): (6) Synthesize what you've learned; identify 2-3 highest-leverage interventions (likely: charter clarification, kill discipline, sponsor-relationship, capability gaps); (7) Share back with team — show you listened, name what you heard, propose direction; invite pushback; (8) Re-establish norms publicly — 'this is how we'll work together'; (9) Make one early visible win — pick a stuck bet and unstick it; or pick a stalled hire and close it. Days 61-90 (Execute): (10) Implement the 2-3 interventions in priority order; (11) Visible quarterly drumbeat — first quarterly artifact under your leadership produced and shared widely; (12) Sponsor relationship locked — written charter clarification or recharter if needed; (13) Build innovation capital with the team — model the moves, take political hits to protect their work, separate idea from person publicly. Note: 90 days is for foundation; real change takes 4-6 quarters. Resist the urge to 'change everything' in the first 30 days — listen first. Reference Watkins's *First 90 Days* (in leadership plugin).
